title: No-Cloning Theorem: Quantum Key Distribution
author: Raja Ram M
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The advent of quantum computing poses a direct and existential threat to this paradigm. Quantum algorithms, most notably Shor's algorithm, are capable of solving the mathematical problems underlying much of today's public-key cryptography with devastating efficiency.2 This has given rise to the "store now, decrypt later" attack vector, where adversaries can harvest and archive encrypted data today with the intent of decrypting it once a fault-tolerant quantum computer becomes available.7 This future vulnerability creates a present-day security crisis for any data that must remain confidential for years or decades. In response, cryptographers have developed Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC), a new generation of classical algorithms based on different mathematical problems believed to be resistant to quantum attacks.1 PQC remains within the paradigm of computational security, offering a vital software-based defense for existing communication networks.
